entry.title: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2025: An Architect’s guide: SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Databricks - BDC263
entry.content:
Hi Everyone, My name is Gaurish Dessai and I’m a Principal Enterprise Architect at SAP. I am part of Global Enterprise Architecture Advisory team which works closely with our customers and on strategic projects. I am a hands-on Enterprise Architect, with over 13+ years of experience in Architecting and delivering programs from idea to adoption. I bring strong expertise in Supply chain, Sales, Digital, M&A, Divestiture, Sustainability & Manufacturing from both customer and consulting background.

My Session: An architect’s guide: SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Databricks solutions - BDC2633. I am presenting together with Michelle Wiktorsson (Databricks) on 20th May 2025 at 1:00 PM - 1:40 PM EDT as part of the SAP Sapphire ASUG Pre-conference in Orlando.

I couldn’t be more excited about this! I started working in the SAP world as a junior SAP Sales & Distribution (SD) functional consultant in 2012. We were a bunch of fresh campus-hire graduates and received 2 months of intense SAP functional and technical training at the learning academy in Hyderabad.

As an junior SAP ‘resource’ working in support and projects from an offshore development center (ODC) for a very large client, attending SAP Sapphire was a distant dream! SAP Sapphire was when the whole ODC would be buzzing about latest launches by SAP for days and followed up by reading about them on SAP Community blog

Since then, 13 SAP Sapphire’s later, the moment came! I got confirmation of our session being selected for SAP Sapphire ASUG Pre-Conference at Orlando, I was over the moon.  It was actually my co-presenter, Michelle's idea to propose a join session on the topic of SAP Business Data Cloud  and Databricks tailored for Architects – and I am glad that we followed through with that idea.

So, here we are, less than 20 days as I write this post and we submit the first draft of our session for review with the ASUG team! In our session, we would be talking about aspects of SAP Business Data Cloud and Databricks which are critical for every architect to know.

What will you learn from the session?

  1. Why SAP Business Data Cloud and Why Now?
  2. What does it mean for architects
  3. What are the capabilities of SAP Business Data and Databricks
  4. Transition paths to modernizing your Data landscape
  5. Architects role in making trusted data available to the business
  6. How Architects can derive value from SAP BDC and Databricks

So, If you’re an architect navigating the evolving data landscape—or just curious about how SAP BDC and Databricks, feel free to drop into our session. Michelle and I are bringing together practical insights, real-world patterns, and a good bit of our combined experience to make it worth your time. Hope to see you there and geek out over data and architecture with us!

Now let’s hear from you - please feel free to share your thoughts in the comments! 🚀 

Please answer these question/s by adding a comment in the comments section (e.g. 1-B,2-C):   

Question 1:   “Which capabilities of SAP Business Data Cloud would you like to understand in detail?”  

Multiple Choice:  

  1. A) Enterprise-wide data integration across SAP and non-SAP environments  
  2. B) Advanced data governance, compliance, and security management  
  3. C) Embedded AI, machine learning, and intelligent analytics capabilities  

Question 2:   “As you prepare your data landscape for AI, which areas are your highest priorities?”  

Multiple Choice:  

  1. A) Driving data quality, consistency, and accessibility at scale  
  2. B) Building a scalable, resilient infrastructure for AI and ML workloads  
  3. C) Strengthening data governance frameworks to enable responsible AI  

Related community tags (Subscribe to remain current): SAP Business Data Cloud 

Related community topic pages: SAP Business Data Cloud 

Link to our session on the SAP Sapphire Session Catalog- https://www.sap.com/events/sapphire/flow/sap/so25/catalog-inperson/page/catalog/session/1742305886714001DI6M

entry.title: SAP Datasphere - Data flow scripts and generic OData - Unpacking nested values
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Datasphere is subset of the new SAP Business Data Cloud offering, which aims to enables every data professional to deliver seamless and scalable access to mission-critical business data.

Datasphere offers a host of connectors to all kinds of sources, SAP and non-SAP alike. One of the most versatile is the "Generic OData" connection, which allows consumption of a wide range of sources.  

In this blog, we will see how we can utilize the Data Flow feature to consume complex OData responses. 

Note: This is a rather niche guide to the Script Operator in Data Flows. If you were looking for more high-level information, I would recommend the official SAP Datasphere Documents, or the Datasphere tag to browse blog posts.

Summary

The Generic OData connector in Datasphere can't "flatten" a nest structure, and will end up storing nested structures as strings. The script operator in Data Flows allows developers to handle complex OData entities with Python, but can be an cumbersome to say the least.

Your best option is to consider some sort of middleware or integration platform between the source and Datasphere, that better allows you to handle OData entities better. 

Prerequisites 

If you want to follow along, you will need:

  1. Access to a Datasphere Tenant
  2. Access to a development space, and the correct authorizations.
  3. A basic idea of Datasphere objects: Data Flows and Local Tables 
  4. An OData source (I will use the OData.org reference services, so you should be able to reproduce the results)
  5. An understanding of the Python module Pandas, and dataframes
  6. A deep and seething hate for Regular Expressions, but still see their value. I also hate regular expressions.  

Disclaimer: I don't like this solution any more than you do

I want to be transparent going into this: I don't think this is a very good or elegant solution. 
In an ideal world, I would prefer some sort of integration platform, CIP or something else, between the OData service and Datasphere to correctly unpack and transform the data.

If you don't have that, you may consider the following. 

Step 1: Establish the connections

In your development space, you can set up a connection to the services.
OData.org exposes both V2 and V4 OData services.

Clicking on the service will provide you with the service URL:

In Datasphere, create a Generic OData Connection:

Step 2: Consume /Suppliers in a Data Flow

Add the /Suppliers to a new Data Flow and preview the data.

Note: You may get an error, that the source tables is not "Deployed". 
If you do, deploy the table and preview the data again.

Step 3 (Optional) : Understanding the problem

If you try to consume the service in a service like postman, or just through a Python request, you will get the following JSON response:

You can see that there are two dictionaries here. Properties, which contain the keys (1) ID, (2) Name, (3) Address, and (4) Concurrency, and then another under Address, which contains Street, City, State, ZipCode and Country. 

There are many ways to interpret this data, but the easiest would be to unpack it into the following table:

IDNameAddress_StreetAddress_CityAddress_StateAddress_ZipCodeCountryConcurrency
0Exotic LiquidsNE 228thSammamishWA98074USA0

As you can see, Address is unpacked into it's four columns, and we get a lovely, simple table. 

However, the table deployed in Step 2, in Datasphere looks like this:

Which is an absolute nightmare. Extracting the data in SQL views (graphical or otherwise) will be substring-hell, as there are no JSON interpreters in the HANA SQLScript.  

Step 4: Add a Script Operator

Alright, now let's transform the data with a Python Script.

Firstly, add a script to your canvas and connect the source.

Step 5: Add the new columns to the script operator

The schema of the script operator is not dynamic. That means you will need to manually add the required columns to the operator:

Step 6: Reorder the columns

The absolute 'funniest' quirk is that the output of the script is based on the order of the columns, not the names / keys.

That means, if your resulting DataFrame has this schema:

IDNameAddress_StreetAddress_CityAddress_StateAddress_ZipCodeCountryConcurrency

The columns must be in the same order.
Otherwise the value of ID will be written to the "State" column.
Fun stuff 🙂

Drag and drop until your columns are in the required order:

Note: This problem may very well be solved in the future, or it may be working-as-designed and always just be a massive pain in the donkey.

Step 7: Let's code away!

Alrighty, so now we have the setup in place, we can begin to create a Python script to solve our problem. 

Script goals

The Script, in simple terms, have to do two things:

  1. Take the JSON string from the input, and convert it to a dictionary or a list of dictionaries. 
  2. Read the values from the result of step 1 and write them to the dataframe. 

Limitations

However, the Python Environment in Datasphere is highly restricted and does not allow for additional imports. 
At the time of writing, the following libraries are supported:

  • time
  • dateutil.parser
  • datetime
  • calendar
  • random
  • math
  • re
  • Pandas (except I/O, like read_csv, write csv, ect.)
  • Numpy (Except I/O like read_file, from_file ect.)
  • buildints (Except I/O like print, breakpoint, input, open, import, exec, eval, ect.)

You can see the full list of supported and disabled features in the documentation.

Step 7.1: The code editor

The base code editor looks like this:

As your may notice, the script takes the form of a function - Transform - that takes one variable: data. The source data will be read just like you see it in the Data Preview, as a Pandas DataFrame called 'data'. 
A section has been marked in two strings of #, which is where you will add your code. 

Note: At the time of writing, the editor has no code-completion, any sort of help or utilities, and does not allow you to place breakpoints or write an output to the console - or anywhere. 
This makes debugging extremely tedious. 

Step 7.2: Script - Convert string to a Dictionary or List object.

The JSON data is stored in Datasphere as a string, and we have to transform that without the JSON module, which would make the exercise trivial. 

So, will do the following: 

  1. Find all the 'key' : 'value' pairs in the data - make sure we can also handle nested dictionaries. 
  2. Loop through the pairs and add them to a dictionary. 

To accomplish step 1, we will use regular expressions that can identify the pairs. The following formats are supported:

  • 'Key' : 'Value' (both with and without whitespaces around the : is supported)
  • "key" : "Value" (both with and without whitespaces around the : is supported)

That is, whether your data uses ' or " is okay. 

r'["\'](\w+)["\']\s*:\s*(\{.*?\}|["\'].*?["\']|\d+)'

Yes, I know it looks like a pocket-calculator having a stroke, but it works. 
You can use the regex-tester to play around with it for yourself.

Python allows a function to be defined within another function, so we can begin to package our logic into a function. 

def process_dict(dict_str):
    # Assume the input string is a dictionary and parse it
    key_val_pattern = r'["\'](\w+)["\']\s*:\s*(\{.*?\}|["\'].*?["\']|\d+)'  # Matches key-value pairs, supporting ' and "
    matches = re.findall(key_val_pattern, dict_str)

The Variable "matches" will contain a list of all the pairs in the input. 

Now, we can loop through the matches and create our dictionary:

def process_dict(dict_str) -> dict:
    # Assume the input string is a dictionary and parse it
    key_val_pattern = r'["\'](\w+)["\']\s*:\s*(\{.*?\}|["\'].*?["\']|\d+)'  # Matches key-value pairs, supporting ' and "
    matches = re.findall(key_val_pattern, dict_str)
    
    parsed_dict = {}
    for key, value in matches:
        # Loop through and sotres keys as lower case, to support case-sentitivity. 
        if value.startswith('{'):  # If the value is a nested dictionary
            # Recursively process the nested dictionary
            parsed_dict[str(key).lower()] = process_dict(value)
        elif value.startswith('"') or value.startswith("'"):  # If the value is a string. 
            parsed_dict[str(key).lower()] = value.strip('"').strip("'")  # Remove quotes (either ' or ")
    
    return parsed_dict

Note in line 9, we which for a nested dictionary and recursively handle that. 

If we pass this string

"{'ID': 0, 'Name': 'Exotic Liquids', 'Address': { 'Street': 'NE 228th', 'City': 'Sammamish', 'State':  WA', 'ZipCode': 98074, 'Country': 'USA' }, 'Concurrency': 0 }"

We will get the following result:

{'id': 0, 'name': 'Exotic Liquids', 'address': { 'street': 'NE 228th', 'city': 'Sammamish', 'state':  WA', 'zipcode': 98074, 'country': 'USA' }, 'concurrency': 0 }

Which we can then use to get the data we need. 

Note: The full script also contains handling for a list of dictionaries. 

Step 7.3: Add the data to the DataFrame

Now that you have your data in a variable, you can manipulate the DataFrame.

There is an infinite way of implementing this, but the most readable is a new function that uses the one above, and extracts the required info column by column. 
There are faster ways, but for the sake of the example, readability > speed. 

def get_address_data(row, newcolumn : str):
    address = row["Address"]
    address_info = process_dict(address)
    return_value = address_info["address"].get(newcolumn.lower(),'')
    return return_value

data["State"] = data.apply(lambda row: get_address_data(row, "State"), axis = 1)

In line 7, we create a new column, State and pass the entire row, and a value we want out. 

Python now iterates through the dataframe and applies the function. 

Step 8: The full script

The full script will then look like this.

def transform(data):
    """
    This function body should contain all the desired transformations on incoming DataFrame. Permitted builtin functions
    as well as permitted NumPy and Pandas objects and functions are available inside this function.
    Permitted NumPy and Pandas objects and functions can be used with aliases 'np' and 'pd' respectively.
    This function executes in a sandbox mode. Please refer the documentation for permitted objects and functions. Using
    any restricted functions or objects would cause an internal exception and result in a pipeline failure.
    Any code outside this function body will not be executed and inclusion of such code is discouraged.
    :param data: Pandas DataFrame
    :return: Pandas DataFrame
    """
    #####################################################
    def parse_string_to_list(data_str):
        result_list = []
        if ( data_str in ['','-','NULL',None] ):
            return result_list
        # Check if the string represents a list
        if data_str.startswith('['):
            # Extract individual elements of the list using regex
            # Matches nested dictionaries or flat dictionaries
            list_pattern = r'\{[^{}]*\{[^{}]*\}[^{}]*\}|\{[^{}]*\}'  
            list_items = re.findall(list_pattern, data_str)
        else:  
        # If it's not a list, treat the whole string as a single dictionary
            list_items = [data_str]

        # Step 2: Process each item in the list (or the single string)
        for item in list_items:
            result_list.append(process_dict(item))

        return result_list

    def process_dict(dict_str) -> dict:
        # Assume the input string is a dictionary and parse it
        key_val_pattern = r'["\'](\w+)["\']\s*:\s*(\{.*?\}|["\'].*?["\']|\d+)'  # Matches key-value pairs, supporting ' and "
        matches = re.findall(key_val_pattern, dict_str)
        
        parsed_dict = {}
        for key, value in matches:
            # Loop through and sotres keys as lower case, to support case-sentitivity. 
            if value.startswith('{'):  # If the value is a nested dictionary
                # Recursively process the nested dictionary
                parsed_dict[str(key).lower()] = process_dict(value)
            elif value.startswith('"') or value.startswith("'"):  # If the value is a string. 
                parsed_dict[str(key).lower()] = value.strip('"').strip("'")  # Remove quotes (either ' or ")
        
        return parsed_dict

    def get_address_data(row, newcolumn : str):
        # Get the value of the "Address" column
        address = row["Address"]
        # Process the input and read the first element of the list
        address_info = parse_string_to_list(address)[0]
        # get the requested value, and return an empty-string 
        return_value = address_info["address"].get(newcolumn.lower(),'')
        
        return return_value

    data["State"] = data.apply(lambda row: get_address_data(row, "State"), axis = 1)
    data["Zip"] = data.apply(lambda row: get_address_data(row, "zipcode"), axis = 1)
    data["Street"] = data.apply(lambda row: get_address_data(row, "Street"), axis = 1)
    data["Country"] = data.apply(lambda row: get_address_data(row, "Country"), axis = 1)
    data["City"] = data.apply(lambda row: get_address_data(row, "City"), axis = 1)
    #####################################################
    return data

Step 9: Add or select target table and run the Data Flow

When you've added a target local table, you can run the data flow, and you should get the following result:

Conclusion

The support for nested OData entities, and python code, in Datasphere is lacking, and will hopefully be expanded greatly in the future. 
Whether you have the option to directly change the OData source, or you are using a public service, you should really consider some sort of middleware between the source and Datasphere. 

Until then, you do have the option to manually transform JSON, stored as strings, in Datasphere, but this can be a massive headache.

entry.title: SAP Datasphere News in April
entry.content:
SAP Datasphere News in April

Another month with three new releases is in the books ✨

We delivered great new features in SAP Datasphere with all April releases. Learn more in my short top features video in April 2025 and the quarterly What’s New session on YouTube 📺

In addition, explore the latest updates in our community news blogs.

Check out the new book about SAP BW modernization: Unlocking SAP BW: Evolution from Legacy to Cloud to learn about the different options for your SAP BW environment in combination with SAP Datasphere or SAP Business Data Cloud.

 

 

My top features in April

 

Analytic Model

Stacking

You can create a new analytic model using another analytic model as fact source and re-use the modelled definitions for measures, exposed attributes, dimensions, and variables.
➡️  More information

Replace The Fact Source of an Analytic Model

You can replace the fact source of an existing analytic model by dragging the new fact source onto the fact source of the analytic model on the canvas.
➡️  More information.

Impact and Lineage Analysis for Measures

You can display an impact and lineage analysis for measures in the analytic model.

Format of Measures

You can now change the format of a measure (decimal places and scale) in an analytic model in the Formatting section in the editor.

 

Data Validation: Referential Integrity

A new validation rule helps you ensure that all values in foreign key columns in fact views are present in the key columns of the associated dimensions.
➡️  More information

 

Task chains: Deep Retry

For task chains that include nested task chains, the behavior for retrying failed tasks within a nested chain has been improved. When a task chain reports a failure, and the failure occurred running one of the tasks inside a nested task chain, retry of the parent task chain no longer requires rerunning all the tasks in the nested task chain, but only needs to rerun tasks from the point where an individual task reported a failure.
➡️  More information

 

Microsoft SQL Server as source in Replication Flow

You can now use connection type Microsoft SQL Server as a source to replicate data from these Microsoft SQL Server databases to all targets in replication flows.
➡️  More information

 

 

 

Quarterly What’s New in SAP Datasphere

We just ran our quarterly What's New in SAP Datasphere session on YouTube on April 29th, 2025! Get the latest updates on features and functions delivered in Q1 2025.

The session covers the following topics 👇
🔹️  General Product Updates
🔹️  Updates in Data Integration
🔹️  Updates in Data Modeling
🔹️  Updates on various other topics
🔹️  Outlook

👉 Watch the recording https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jq2oDD40uyQ

 

 

SAPPHIRE

It is already May and that is SAP SAPPHIRE time. We also have great coverage with more than 30 sessions on SAP Business Data Cloud including SAP Datasphere by product managers, executives, customers, and partners covering all areas of BDC including roadmap sessions, demos, and strategy talks. Customers and partners will share their experience and if you are keen on chatting with experts drop by our booth area with SAP Business Data Cloud. We are also offering first hands-on experience as part of the ASUG pre-conference in case you want to get your hands on it.

🔗  ASUG pre-conference seminar - Mastering SAP BTP: Drive Digital Transformation with Hands-on Experience - ASUG101
🔗  SAP Business Data Cloud track on the session catalog

 

More blogs to check out 👇

  • SAP Datasphere CLI & Python: Exporting Modeling Objects to CSV Files for Each Artifact
  • SAP Datasphere - Data flow scripts and generic OData - Unpacking nested values
  • A demo to consume MySQL database in Datasphere with Generic JDBC connection
  • SAP Sapphire Orlando 2025: An Architect’s guide: SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Databricks - BDC263
  • SAP Datasphere External Access Overview: APIs, CLI and SQL
  • Building Intelligent Data Applications with SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine
  • Seamless Planning with Datasphere & SAC: Seamless Planning in the Cloud?
  • Get Hands-On Workshops with SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Business Data Cloud : Expert Guided Implementation series
  • SAP Business Data Cloud - A Complete Multi-Architecture Platform?
  • Managing planning across global and local structures with SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Run APL on SAP Data Products of Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Business Data Cloud and what it means for SAP Datasphere
  • 3 Reasons to Register for the Modernizing SAP BW with SAP Business Data Cloud Webinar
  • Generic OData Service - Datasphere Integration
  • Data Doesn’t Drive the Business - Trust Does. SAP Business Data Cloud Delivers
  • Access SAP HANA Cloud Database Underneath of SAP Datasphere with a Database user
  • Automation Alone Is Not a Strategy: Here’s Why
  • ETL Objects in SAP Datasphere and Dataflows Example
  • SAP Business Data Fabric: Unifying Your Enterprise Data with BDC (Business Data Cloud)
  • Three Reasons SAP BusinessObjects Still Matters in 2025
  • The Next Decade of Enterprise Technology: Are We Ready?
  • Data Access Control in Data Builder and Authorization Scenario in Business Builder - SAP Datasphere 2
  • Data Access Control in Data Builder and Authorization Scenario in Business Builder - SAP Datasphere 1

 

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic page for SAP Datasphere. You will find further product information on our Community with various subpages about Business Content, the SAP BW Bridge as well as content for Best Practices & Troubleshooting. Also check out the new support content for SAP Datasphere on SAP Help for troubleshooting and analysis guides, how-to guides, technical details, and more.

Find out how to unleash the power of your business data with SAP’s free learning content on SAP Datasphere. It’s designed to help you enrich your data projects, simplify the data landscape, and make the most out of your investment. Check out even more role-based learning resources and opportunities to get certified in one place on  SAP Learning site.

entry.title: Next Generation - IT Operating Model (SAP Centric)
entry.content:
This blog aims to define the future Information Technology Operating Model, focusing on governance, agility and value.  It does not focus on SAP RISE, Clean Core or AI, although they will play a major role in your future Enterprise IT Operating Model (especially AIAgent).  "Who is the blog for?" Everyone who is involved in the lifecycle of SAP Systems and ancillary technologies, as it outlines the approach to Solution Architecture, along with online resources, methodologies, and learning assets, to define and reinforce what your future Target Operating Model should look like.  Your operating model and IT capability need to transition from primarily  development, security and operations toward Analytics, Data products, Integration and Development.  Which means you and your colleagues also need to shift their focus, reusing existing skills, and gaining new ones, so that everyone is empowered with the ability to add value.

"Why?"  Because owning, securing and operating your own systems is both sunk and prospective costs.  Creating value by productising your own data, putting it to work with analytical applications and AI, and extending processes to suit your business needs, all have a direct impact on net earnings.  "So what? This is not new or noteworthy, it's just common sense, there are however, entry level and transition barriers, for instance, does this mean all employees need to be data scientists and developers?"  Well, yes and no.  SAP has gone to great lengths to lower the skill entry level threshold, with platforms and tools designed with affordance and AI features, such as the SAP Build Family which embodies Low Code / No Code (LCNC) concepts, On-Stack with S/4HANA with Key User Extensibility, Side-by-side with SAP Business Technology Platform, and much more, so that your workforce can intuitively discover and realise the digital experience that best works for them.  Which brings us back to the aim of this blog 'to set out the steps to adapt your Information Technology Operating Model.'

There's some big statements in the first two paragraphs, so let's recap and dig a little deeper.

  • Analytics Data Integration and Development Value:
    • Organisations need to adapt their DevSecOps IT Operating Model to align with adding business value.  
  • The IT Operating Model is a continually evolving target:
    • Adoption
    • Integration
    • Compliance
    • Continuous innovation
    • Workforce enablement
    • A core principle and strategic objective of, future proof IT

Synopsis

Organizations have, and continue, to invest heavily in their digital workforce.  The operations team: network, operating system, database administrators, the development team: frontend, integration and backend development, the security and compliance teams, project managers, architects, and the various application and data support teams, the list goes on.  These teams are instrumental in keeping the company running, however generational knowledge is often lost, even if it's been carefully curated and catalogued.

As organisations transition to the cloud, it is tempting to maintain the status-quo, due in part, to difficulty in change management or the unquantified risk, such as having to 'reinvent the wheel'.  This leads organisations adopting a 'lift and shift' approach, with the assumption that, change will somehow be minimised.  This approach can be achieved using existing skills but it adds little to no value, and the software architecture is already constrained by the sunset of various products.

In adopting SAP RISE, a large proportion of the application stack transitions from your organisations IT teams to SAP and its Partners, this does not mean that your organisation will not need an operations team, it does mean that they will have to refocus toward service management, monitoring, and governance, risk and compliance.  Development teams will have less technical freedom with an increase in Extension Technology per Extension Task, therefore the need for 'Pro Code' developers will reduce, whilst the 'Key Users Extensibility' (formerly 'Citizen Developer') will increase.  This will require strong development governance (see previous point).  The skills threshold for data and analytics has also reduced, comprising of four distinct entities each with their own features and capabilities.  Your organisation needs to productise data so that it can be consumed by the analytics platform of your choice, and SAP AI capabilities.  More than 70% of integration projects fail, so organisations need all the help they can get.

SAP has recognised that strong integration governance is instrumental in constraining complexity and cost, responding with a robust template based Integration Solution Advisory Methodology to reduce "tight coupling between business applications and increasing resilience."  Security and compliance will shift, from an organisation being solely responsible, to a shared responsibility model.  This will require auditing, and your organisation will have to manage and facilitate this.

Enterprise Architecture

A foreword on Enterprise Architecture (EA).  I see EA in terms of a recipe book, with some being are better than others, but it's all a matter of taste, right?  TOGAF, ZACHMANN, FEAF, Gartner and SAP EA Methodology, each have unique strengths and approaches for aligning IT with the business, for the remainder of this blog, and for simplification of context I'll compare only to the SAP EA and TOGAF (interchangeably) as they are closest in alignment.  The operating model is an interpretation of one or more recipes to produce a culinary experience.  I'm not suggesting that every experience is good, so it's worthwhile starting with something that’s 'proven' (continuing the culinary metaphor).  In other words The IT Operating Model sets out the practical steps to execute the Enterprise Architecture (vision).  Enterprise Architecture has three core concerns which are addressed in 9 common use cases:

  • Promote Growth
    • Monoliths to microservices, cloud transformation, IoT Architecture
  • Reduce complexity
    • Post merger integration, application rationalisation, integration architecture
  • Ensure compliance
    • Technology risk management, data compliance, governance standards

"So, how do the core concerns of EA translate to the IT Operating Model?"

The IT Operating Model

Gartner defines the outcomes for the IT Operating Model (ITOM) as:

  1. Enable IT efficiency. For example, run projects and efficiently keep the business running.
  2. Enhance business performance, such as improved external customer experience, improved user productivity or cost optimization through business process automation.
  3. Transform the business by creating enterprise wide competitive advantage or growth.

IT Operating Model: How CIOs Show Its Value | Gartner

Mapping EA to ITOM

Well it's not a 1:1 mapping, in fact I would be doing it a disservice to even try, because IT Operating Models are fundamentally about people, whereas EA focuses predominantly on things, or in TOGAF terms ' Requirements Management'.  EA is a top down approach from strategic objectives through to infrastructure and network architecture, and ITOMs are commonly viewed as pyramids or as opposing forces of; cost, quality and flexibility (but you can only really favour two).  Operations, security and development are cross topics touching each of the 8 layers of Enterprise Architecture:

  1. Business Capabilities - Business Architecture
  2. Business Processes - Processes Architecture
  3. Functional Capabilities - Functional Architecture
  4. Application & Information - Application & Information Architecture
  5. Software Components - Software & Service Architecture
  6. Integration Platforms - Integration Architecture
  7. Data - Physical Data Architecture
  8. Infrastructure Cloud - Infrastructure and Network Architecture

IT Architecture, concerns itself with Functional through to Infrastructure, Technology Architecture, Software through to Infrastructure, and Enterprise Architecture focuses strategically on the whole stack.  I've gone the long way around this (explanation), but we needed to be on the same page so that it is clear what is changing.  To a large degree layers 4 to 8 are managed and operated by SAP and its Partners within the various RISE models, alleviating the load on your organisations; operations, development and security teams.  Meaning:

  • Technology Architecture is now concerned primarily with Integration and Data Governance
  • IT Architecture is concerned with Functional requirements, and customisation governance
    • This is where most of the magic happens
  • Enterprise Architecture derives strategic objectives, mapping them to Business Capabilities and Processes whilst maintaining cohesion with existing policies and platforms.

On the other hand, your organizations Development, Security & Operations (DevSecOps) teams should be turning their attention to layers 1 through 5 (Business Capabilities to Software components)

  • Development - Less technical freedom with an increase in Extension Technology per Extension Task
  • Security - Reduced focus on technology, and an increase on compliance
  • Operations - Reduced focus on technology and an increase on monitoring and service management

It starts to become clear at this point why SAP has gone to the efforts they have, to reduce the skill entry threshold for development(extension), integration and data and analytics, as this is the where the IT Operating Model really starts to contribute value.  To help with this challenge, SAP has provided methodologies for each of the following domains:

  • Data and Analytics
  • Integration
  • Extension

And the SAP Trust Centre https://www.sap.com/australia/about/trust-center.html is now the primary online resource for your security and compliance teams in the shared responsibility model. 

These methodologies are not a 'paint with numbers' on how to how to create analytics dashboards, or extending business processes, you can find tutorials, missions, and reference architectures on https://developers.sap.com/ and https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/ which also includes, service and AI estimates and catalogues.  These methodologies are phased approaches, with ease entry characteristics, meaning your current IT Architecture maturity need not be barrier, in fact it can be an asset.  Each of the methodologies comprises of phases and steps, to facilitate organisations to leverage their current architectural assets that can be reverse engineered (backwards) or engineered (forwards) to gradually introduce stronger governance and reducing complexity.

For reference some of the SAP components are described below, with some links for further reading:

SAP Business Technology Platform

Simplistically the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) is a global platform of platforms (PaaS), and software solutions (SaaS), enabling (side-by-side) custom development, persistence (storage), automation, and AI capabilities, that can be leveraged and integrated with other SAP Software, Cloud and On-Premise.

A little more detail.  The SAP BTP also benefits from open standards to facilitate integration to non SAP software using pre-built, and features-rich connectors (80+ standard adapters, 170+ non-SAP connectors), de-risking integration efforts, reducing complexity and cost.  All underpinned by a foundational global, regional and subaccount model with a clear separation of concerns, such as data, identity and authentication.

More still.  There are three environments (Platform as a Service (PaaS)) available at the SAP BTP subaccount level; Kyma, ABAP, and Cloud Foundry, this is where side-by-side extensibility is achieved, with each having its own user and authorisation environment management.  This may sound onerous, however once you unpack the use cases, not only does it make sense, it gives your organisation the ability to choose the most appropriate Extension Technology per Task.  In my view this is 'cost optimised flexibility', if you recall my earlier statement in the section Mapping EA to ITOM above, 'ITOMs are commonly viewed as pyramids or as opposing forces of cost, quality and flexibility (but you can only really favour two)', your organisation can increase flexibility with SAP BTP as it is builtin, without significantly impacting cost, or quality.

HINT - Project managers you can add this last point to your list of intangible benefits.

GROWTH - Solution Architects learn about the various Enterprise Agreements, Environments and Runtimes at

https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/3019

SAP Data and Analytics

Engulfed by the recent announcement of SAP Business Data Cloud SAP there are three product capabilities layers targeted at 4 users types:

  • Analytics and Planning
    • Application Users
  • Business Data Fabric
    • Analysts and Data modeller
  • Database layer
    • Administrators/developers

The traditional administrator role within operations needs to refocuses their efforts on capacity planning (storage), the ingress of structured, semi-structured and unstructured data, and facilitating security and privacy.

GROWTH - Administrators growth opportunity, learn SAP Datasphere security and capacity planning.  https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/4104

Data modellers and analysts are primarily focused on the capabilities of SAP Datasphere and this naturally lands within the Data Fabric layer along with Master Data Management.  The aim of SAP Datasphere is to simplify your data landscape, whilst enriching data so that value can be extracted, this is productising data, and adding it to your Datasphere Catalogue.

COLLABORATE - Data modellers and analysts will be required to work closely with administrators to organise and control the level of visibility and collaboration of SAP Datasphere Spaces.

Application Users, this is where we see some of the specialist skills, traditionally the realm of Analysts and Data Modellers start to transition to 'Key Users' (formerly 'Citizen Developers'), with the help of generative AI to discover hidden insights, automate reporting and develop business plans with Joule Copilot.  Application users work mainly in SAP Analytics Cloud using simple UI elements to create 'Stories' and 'Analytic Applications' (lowered skill threshold).  SAP Analytics Cloud is a native SAP BTP SaaS product.

GROWTH - Application Users (Key Users) growth opportunity to learn the many SAP Analytics Cloud planning tools.  https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/3091/

SAP Integration Suite

The SAP Integration Suite is a set of capabilities rather than products, which collectively enable organisations to; Manage APIs, and business events, extend non-SAP connectivity, and more…  These capabilities are:

  • Cloud Integration
  • API ManagementGraph
  • Open Connectors
  • Integration Advisor
  • Trading Partner Management
  • Integration Assessment
  • Migration Assessment
  • SAP Event Mesh

The idea of an Application Factory, and Centre of Excellence comes from the SAPs Integration Strategy which suggests organisations maintain an 'Application Factory' paired with an 'Integration Centre of Excellence' to drive the following key imperatives:

  • A Platform Approach.
  • Agility and Scalability.
  • Integration Democratization.
  • Smart Lifecycle Management.
  • Analytics and Predictive Intelligence.

These 5 points are a little abstract, they are introduced here as key concepts, not just for integration, but in terms of Cross Platform Architecture and the Next Generation IT Operations Model to drive value of data products, analytics; stories and dashboards, and business process extensions by levelling up governance with a platform style approach.

The SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology contains a list of common integration roles, which are geared toward large enterprises.  My view is that the Factory and CoE need not be focused solely only on integration, and as such the role description can drop the title of integration:

  • Chief Integration Officer = Chief Information Officer
  • Integration Administrator = Administrator
  • Integration Developer = Developer
  • Integration Architect = Solution Architect
  • Enterprise Architect
  • Application/API Developer = Developer

GROWTH - Administrators, developers and architects should familiarise themselves with the SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology Overview | SAP Help Portal along with the 100+ missions in the SAP Discovery Center Search - integrate

GROWTH - Executives can gain an understanding of SAP Integration Strategy in the White Paper Integrate to Innovate

With business and user roles of:

  • Business Domain Expert
  • Business User
  • Citizen Integrator = Key User

You could argue that some of these roles need to be integration specific, and that I have either devalued, or underplayed the skills required, and of course you'd be right depending on your organisational context.  But looking through a lens of industrialisation, do you need an integration developer 100% of the time?  I'd argue that most organisations need a developer who can scope integration domains, styles and patterns, in line with the well documented SAP methodology, and an integration developer could be augmented when required to create new integration assets for the Catalogue of Integration Use Patterns.

COLLABORATE - Solution Architects will need to work between your organisations Application Factory and Centre of Excellence to ensure current and evolving business requirements are serviceable by your organisations IT capabilities.

entry.title: Become an Early Adopter for Joule in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Are you ready to explore what’s next in data innovation?

The Early Adopter Care Program for Joule in SAP Datasphere is now open, offering the chance to get an early look and hands-on experience with our latest AI-powered capabilities before general release. As a participant, you’ll get exclusive first access to Joule capabilities, starting with features like space management and natural language access to all available SAP Datasphere documentation; all through an Early Adopter Care program run in close collaboration with SAP.

Ready to join?

You'll find everything you need to get started below — including program details, prerequisites, and how to sign up.

 

What is in for you?

🔹Get a quick start on Joule in SAP Datasphere
🔹Preferred message handling
🔹Dedicated development contact and project coach who will stay in contact with the project lead
🔹Option to influence direction of future releases with your feedback

 

When is it happening and how to register?

🔹Registration is open starting today
🔹Earliest Start Date:[1] May 15, 2025 (planned)
🔹Early Adopter Care Program Lead: Stefan Schares

Join us as a Customer or Partner: participation will be confirmed upon review/approval of the project scope completed in the following online form.[2]

➡️  Sign up here: using the online scoping document form.

 

What should I consider before registering?

Participation in the Early Adopter Care program is voluntary and free of charge, at the sole discretion of SAP, and dependent on the following prerequisites, i.a.:

  1. For existing SAP Datasphere customers
  2. Plan to use Joule in SAP Datasphere in production
  3. Willingness to become a customer reference.
  4. Are open to give regular feedback to our product management or customer success team?

 

 

[1] If you experience issues with this site or the associated links, please try using the Chrome browser

[2] Future developments are at the discretion of SAP and may be changed at any time for any reason without notice

entry.title: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2025: SAP and Databricks open a bold new era of data and AI - BDC2767
entry.content:
At this year’s SAP Sapphire, I’m excited to share groundbreaking innovations that are changing the way organizations leverage data. Join me to explore how SAP and Databricks are coming together to transform enterprise data strategies and accelerate the path to AI.

📅Session Details:

Title:
Unleashing the Value of Business Data with SAP Business Data Cloud and Databricks

When:

  • Tuesday, May 20 – 2:00 PM to 2:20 PM
  • Wednesday, May 21 – 1:30 PM to 1:50 PM

Where:
SAP Sapphire – Session Link

What You’ll Learn:

SAP and Databricks announced a landmark partnership back in February that will redefine how applications and data platforms work together. From curated SAP data products to zero-copy integration, this session will show how SAP Databricks within the SAP Business Data Cloud solution empowers data professionals to:

  • Accelerate the development and deployment of AI models and data applications
  • Unlock trusted, business-context-rich data without duplication
  • Seamlessly connect SAP data to the Databricks Lakehouse Platform
  • Deliver real-time, intelligent insights that drive value across the enterprise

Whether you're responsible for data strategy, analytics, or innovation, this session will equip you with actionable insights to elevate your organization’s data capabilities.

Co-Speaker: I am excited to be joined by former colleague and longtime friend Brian Suk, a Senior Solutions Architect at Databricks.

About Me: Kara Reed
I’m a Senior Director of Product Marketing at SAP, where I focus on helping organizations adopt and maximize the value of SAP Business Data Cloud and other cloud-based data solutions. Over the past 14 years at SAP, I’ve held a variety of roles in the Data & Analytics organization, most recently on the Retail Solution Management team. I work closely with customers and industry leaders to guide our go-to-market strategies and support the data transformation journey.

The fun doesn't stop there! Check out these other sessions to learn more about this game changing partnership and the value SAP Databricks within SAP Business Data Cloud brings!

An architect’s guide: SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Databricks solutions - BDC2633

  • Tuesday, May 20 1:00-1:30

SAP Databricks: Simplify analytics and AI for your SAP software data - PAR1122 – customer success story

  • Tuesday, May 20 1:30-1:50

How Baker Hughes modernizes its applications with SAP Business Data Cloud - BDC3260 – customer success story

  • Tuesday, May 20 2:30-2:50

Unlock the potential of AI with the SAP Databricks solution - PAR1123 – customer success story

  • Wednesday, May 21 11:00-11:20

Can't make it to SAP Sapphire? No problem! Follow along the tags and topic pages below to stay up to date on the latest SAP data and analytics news!

Tags: Data and Analytics SAP Datasphere SAP Business Data Cloud

Topic pages:  Data and Analytics  SAP Business Data Cloud  SAP Datasphere

Now I would like to hear from you:

  • Which use cases in your business could be accelerated or simplified by leveraging the zero-copy approach of SAP Databricks?

entry.title: Case Study: Renovating a Data Inventory
entry.content:
Many times you may be instructed to create a new application to replace an existing one. Your agency might have taken over a legacy group, you may be a new hire after the last group quit, or you might want to leverage prior art instead of starting from scratch.

In this case, I needed to upgrade a forest inventory in order to better work with landscapers and architects/engineers, where previous data were on paper or disorganized digital media. While this scope covers a few acres/hectares, the design concept should fit a bigger coverage area such as a metropolis, a public park, or private holdings. Basically, an operations and maintenance (O&M) guidance document to further natural resource protection.

In an earlier iteration, the solution was to use a GIS mapping layer, add points or polygons per tree, and sprinkle advice to the sawyers ("dead limb to be removed"). I quickly realized that naming each tree was a poor choice for a unique identifier. The "north cherry tree" and the "south cherry tree" seemed self-evident until the assigned worker had no compass-sense. Rather than starting from scratch, I searched for examples. And if you've used the big search engines lately, you'll understand this may not be easy or rewarding.

The basic requirements were: (1) tree (or shrubbery) species list, (2) measured or observed conditions, and (3) a maintenance or care plan per tree. In a large-scale version, there would be multiple crews collecting the first 2 parts, and maybe a different set of crews caring for the trees. To avoid data getting stale, the maintenance crew sends the feedback to the inventory so the next cycle has current state.

One interesting site I found with templates + instructions was a state university, connected to federal government services  (USDA - Department of Agriculture). There was an Excel sheet (two, in fact, one blank and one populated exemplar), an instruction document in MS Word format, and links. I grabbed the files and put on the detective hat to discern the relationships and design created. Somewhat surprisingly, the files are 25 years old yet quite usable. For my renovation, the content would be stored in a database not a spreadsheet, meaning I needed to closely inspect the referential integrity and grow the design to include better data validation, and add any missing or desired elements.

Primary key

At this point in the redesign, a few keys needed to be documented. Each tree would get a unique serial number, which seemed to be the only required differentiator. While every tree belongs to a specific species, those might not be known initially so that column needed to allow nulls (or "undefined"/"unidentified").

Trees/Codes/Sizes

The examples used a 2-letter code that represented a genus + species, with a lookup table included in a separate spreadsheet tab. In the design steps, this is where I needed to decide to keep that legacy style, or try to enhance this by using the full name. For expediency (which might backfire later when scaling up), I stuck with the 2 letter code. We'd need to train the data collectors what letters to use to translate the "English" name to the Latin name to the coded index value. Presumably a later application layer would make the lookups easier with pull-down selections. But for now, stick with the inherited design.

As it turned out, under 100 species were listed. In a diverse forest, there might be more than this sometimes, but for urban or monoculture (planted forests), you might find fewer than 10 predominant. Seemed manageable, so I looked more closely at the lookup table. The first hurdle, the primary key, was unique. While you might have expected this result, you should check anyway, given the looseness of spreadsheet constraints. OK, 70 codes, including 1 for "unknown". The next check was whether these codes translated 1:1 into a Latin species name. Unfortunately, but not too unsurprisingly, I found duplicates.

Secondary keys

Using typical UNIX command line tools, find the errors:

$ sort   /tmp/species-latin.txt | uniq -c |  grep -v "^ *1 "
      2 Populus grandidentata
      2 Quercus alba

To make the lookup table functional, we'd need to drop (or deprecate) one of these codes.

$ egrep "Populus grandidentata|Quercus alba"  /tmp/code+species.txt
PD      Populus grandidentata
PI      Populus grandidentata
QA      Quercus alba
QC      Quercus alba

In a "green-field" scenario, we'd pick one and just delete the other code. But in a "brown-field" situation where the new logic needs to accommodate history data inventories, we would need to review the past and decide how to react. The legacy sample does have both "PD" and "PI", alas.

Tree inventory, auto-filtered
 

In the new inventory, I would eliminate the above 2 duplicates and add a unique index on the species name, to avoid repeating the mistake.

Next key

Few among us other than trained arborists have good working Latin skills so to make the inventory more friendly, I would add the translated English name, at least for those species known to be within the focus area. I will leave out the gnarly details of verifying each name other than to mention the 70-some species list was missing rather common values, and included ones unlikely to be found here.

Here is where another, unexpected, set of issues came up. Taxonomy of trees and shrubs is a well-established branch (no pun intended), and Latin spelling should probably not vary. Yes, there are changes to the lists as new forms are found (or created), or species thought to differ are found to coincide. But, like searching for an app design in the first place, name searches did not routinely results in a simple answer.

One presumed wrong:

PE      Prunus serontina

The correct spelling appears to be "Prunus serotina" [see, e.g. https://www.wildflower.org/plants/result.php?id_plant=prse2 ]. For future revisions, the USDA code for species has 5 characters, not 2. Might be handy to add those to the lookup table. For now, edit the table.

Another misspelling in the source data is "Tsuga" instead of "Thuja". Unclear how these errors crept in (I blame the squirrels).

$ egrep "Tsuga|Thuja" /tmp/code+species.txt
TD      Tsuga canadensis
TO      Tsuga occidentalis

In this case, the search algorithm showed helpful results rather than wrong or no answers ("Did you mean: Thuja occidentalis [?]").

psql:create_....sql:40: 
ERROR:  duplicate key value violates unique constraint "species_pkey"

Further

With the main keys/constraints designed, or rehabbed, the remaining O&M content fell out fairly easily.

Tree status / condition data

The "planting location" was designed to show whether a specific tree was impacted by paved areas, such as being planted in a hole in a sidewalk. The spreadsheet model used 4 columns, but for simplicity, I'd condense this to a single column with lookup values checked against a list. While an SAP focused solution would have the database in HANA, or Sybase, I used PostgreSQL. Each platform might have different rules or syntax to create constraints. In my case, the "<@" array operator didn't do what I thought the docs said, and I got errors. I chased these down for a bit, then backed off and used an expression that worked. Note that I didn't set syntax errors on the first method, but in testing found the constraints were inoperative.

[...]
  condition bpchar(1) constraint condition_code CHECK (
    condition = ' ' or
    condition = 'G' or
    condition = 'F' or
    condition = 'P' or
    condition = 'D' or
    condition = 'C' or
    condition is NULL ),

One downside to putting the constraint in the table create statement is altering the values later is more work. The other approach is to put the foreign key values into another table. You decide which approach to use based on data knowledge. For this "report card" scenario, I used the old mantra "Every Good Boy Does Fine" (E-G-B-D-F). Or was that a tune?

Other characteristics the inventory will capture are specific conditions, as opposed to general health, so a tree with overhanging wires can be marked as such. These are distinct, and multiple can be true at once, so leave those as individual True/False columns.

Fixes

The inventory crew may or may not know what fixes are needed for every situation, so the original design has a "C" for consult code, meaning ask an expert how to treat or correct a problem. Then, the initial record can be updated to show the maintenance plan. I reasoned the supplied options were adequate, though I am considering adding a "Poison ivy" flag given the propensity of this pest.

Maintenance needs + comments

Similar to overlapping hazards, the maintenance plan may show multiple fixes needed per item, so these need to be separate columns for ease of use. The legacy spreadsheet condensed the titles, making the intent hard to discern; the flags are: Clean, Raise, Reduce, Remove. On first glance, these are unrelated meaning no other constraints are needed. But looking a second time, the "Remove" flag would obviate the need for "Reduce" to be marked, so maybe more intricate triggers are useful.

Legacy markers

The website where I found these resources says it was last updated in 2004; the downloaded files are around 5 years older than that. Not unusual but at least the data can be accessed by current software rather than needing to find a conversion process.

Inventory template properties

Sheet from 1999

Tree Inventory Docs

Links

  • https://www.umass.edu/urbantree/inventory.shtml 
  • https://www.umass.edu/urbantree/mcti/index.htm
  • https://library.weconservepa.org/library_items/593-Street-and-Park-Tree-Inventory-Templates-with-Instructions

entry.title: SAP BDC for Dummies
entry.content:
In recent past, the enterprise data market has moved at a disruptive rate from the outside via new players like Databricks, Snowflake, and an endless barrage of buzzword-laden solutions like artificial intelligence.

Moreover, tackling non-SAP data has been a big missing piece for SAP’s solution till date.

SAP's Business Data Cloud announcement was one of the most ambitious in SAP’s history. It also raised a flurry of questions on SAP's data strategy, how existing investments fit in, and what this means for SAP Business AI. Time to dig into the top community questions.

 

What is SAP Business Data Cloud?

SAP BDC is a fully managed SaaS platform that integrates:

  • SAP Datasphere for data modelling and integration
  • SAP Analytics Cloud for analytics and planning
  • SAP Business Warehouse capabilities (private cloud edition only)
  • Managed Databricks for advanced data processing (with limited features)
  • Context-aware intelligent applications for AI-driven insights (SAP Joule & Databrick's Mosaic)

 

It also includes curated, business-ready data products from SAP’s line-of-business applications which will be fully managed by SAP. Custom Data products can be created by Copying the SAP provided Data products or creating the partner products from Scratch.

 

 Why is SAP launching BDC Now?

  • AI and Data Convergence: The explosion of AI capabilities demands better data infrastructure. BDC is SAP’s answer to this convergence.
  • Enterprise Complexity: Businesses are more interconnected than ever. BDC helps manage this complexity by offering a holistic view of operations.
  • Competitive Pressure: Companies need real-time insights to stay ahead. BDC enables faster, smarter decision-making.
  • Cloud Maturity: Organizations are now more comfortable with cloud-first strategies, making the timing ideal for a cloud-native data platform

 

What problems does SAP BDC solve?

 SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) addresses several critical challenges that modern enterprises face in managing and leveraging their data. Here's a breakdown of the key problems it solves:

  1. Data Fragmentation Across Systems

Problem: Enterprises often have data scattered across SAP systems, third-party applications, and data lakes, making it hard to get a unified view.

BDC Solution: It integrates data from SAP and non-SAP sources into a single, governed environment—eliminating silos and enabling holistic insights.

2. Complex and Costly Data Integration

Problem: Traditional data integration is time-consuming, expensive, and often requires heavy IT involvement.

BDC Solution: Offers pre-built connectors, semantic models, and business-context-aware data products that simplify and accelerate integration.

3. Poor AI and Analytics Readiness

Problem: AI models often fail due to poor data quality, lack of context, or disconnected data sources.

BDC Solution: Ensures high-quality, contextual, and business-ready data that’s ideal for training AI models and generating actionable insights.

4. Limited Access for Business Users

Problem: Business users rely heavily on IT for data access and reporting, slowing down decision-making.

BDC Solution: Empowers business users with self-service analytics, planning tools, and intuitive interfaces—reducing dependency on IT.

5. Governance and Compliance Risks

Problem: Managing data governance, lineage, and compliance across multiple platforms is complex and risky.

BDC Solution: Provides centralized governance, lineage tracking, and policy enforcement to ensure compliance and data trust.

6. Slow Innovation and Time-to-Value

Problem: Building new data-driven applications or insights takes too long due to fragmented tools and processes.

BDC Solution: Speeds up innovation with a unified platform that supports rapid development, testing, and deployment of data products and AI solutions.

Apart from these, data movement is another crucial issue. Customers don't have the same data strategy; experts don't even agree on whether AI data should be in the cloud, on the edge, or in some elaborate 'lakehouse' or 'AI speed layer.' For BDC to live up to its billing, it can't require customers to be moving their data all over the place.

 

What Are Data Products in SAP BDC?

In SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), data products are curated, reusable, and governed datasets that are designed to be easily consumed by business users, analysts, and AI applications. They are a core concept in BDC’s data-as-a-product approach.

A data product in BDC is:

  • A packaged dataset with business context, metadata, and governance rules.
  • Built from SAP and non-SAP data sources.
  • Designed to be discoverable, shareable, and reusable across the organization.
  • Delivered with semantic meaning, so users understand what the data represents.

 

 

What Does "Zero Copy" Mean in BDC?

In traditional data architectures, sharing or analysing data often involves copying it from one system to another—which is time-consuming, costly, and introduces governance risks.

In BDC, "zero copy" means:

  • Data consumers (like analytics tools or AI models) can access data directly from its source.
  • There’s no need to replicate or move the data into another environment.
  • This is enabled through open protocols like Delta Sharing, which allow secure, performant access to data in-place

How It Works

  • Delta Sharing (from Databricks) is integrated into BDC.
  • It allows remote access to Delta Lake tables (stored as Parquet files with metadata) without copying them.
  • BDC uses this to serve curated data products to consumers while maintaining central governance and control.

 

Benefits of Zero Copy in BDC

  1. Faster Access: No waiting for data to be moved or transformed.
  2. Lower Costs: Reduces storage and compute costs by avoiding duplication.
  3. Better Governance: Centralized control over data access and usage.
  4. Real-Time Insights: Enables near real-time analytics and AI applications.

 

I hope this blog gave you a better understanding of SAP BDC now.

How SAP tackles the upcoming challenges with BDC remains to be seen.

 

Happy Learning.

 

Swati Gawade-Shingade.

Cognizant technology Solutions.

entry.title: New Webinar Series: Architecting Your SAP Business Data Cloud Journey – From Data to Intelligence!
entry.content:
Whether you’re just starting your SAP BDC journey or thinking about it as you are reading this, our 8 part webinar series is packed with insights from top SAP leaders, industry experts, and special guest speaker!

🌟Series Overview: Architecting Your SAP BDC Journey – From Data to Intelligence

  • Time: All sessions would be conducted at 11AM EST or 5PM CET
  • Where: Online (Register Now — https://go4.events.sap.com/shift-eaa-bdc-webinar/en_us/home.html )

Check out the complete list of sessions below:

Sessions

Date & Time

Speakers

Check out the details of each of the sessions below:

Individual Session Details

📅Session 1: Data Inspired – Successful Concepts for the Data-Driven Organization

Session 1

We kick off the session with inspiration from our special guest Dr. Sebastian Wernicke, a renowned author of ‘Data Inspired’ and a TED speaker to talk about how organizations can truly become Data Inspired in the age of AI.

  • When: June 3, 2025, at 11 AM EST / 5 PM CET
  • Speakers:
    • Guest Speaker: Dr. Sebastian Wernicke (Partner at Oxera & Author of “Data Inspired”)
    • Paul Kurchina (SAP Enterprise Architecture Community Evangelist)
    • Helmut Grimm (Head of Global Enterprise Architecture & Advisory)
    • Gaurish Dessai (Principal Enterprise Architect, SAP)

Session 1 Recap: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-architecture-blog-posts/session-1-recap-amp-highlights-data-inspired-successful-concepts-for-data/ba-p/14122990

📅Session 2: Unleash Your Data and AI with Irfan Khan and Timo Elliott

We are joined by Irfan Khan to talk about SAP’s Data and AI strategy across its Business Applications. Irfan will then join Timo for a fireside chat to answer questions from the audience and explore how SAP’s latest data and AI strategies can supercharge your organization’s future.

  • When: June 5, 2025, at 11 AM EST / 5 PM CET
  • Speakers:
    • Paul Kurchina
    • Irfan Khan (President & Chief Product Officer, SAP Data & Analytics)
    • Timo Elliott (Global Innovation Evangelist & VP Marketing at SAP)

Session 2 Recap: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-architecture-blog-posts/session-2-recap-amp-highlights-unleash-your-data-and-ai-with-irfan-khan-and/ba-p/14123022

📅Session 3: Unpacking SAP Business Data Cloud for Enterprise Architects

 

Next, we unravel SAP Business Data Cloud and Databricks capabilities for Architects and understand what’s under the hood. We do a technical deep dive into how SAP BDC fits into your landscape. In this session, we will provide architects with a complete view of SAP’s new data offering called SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC).  SAP BDC allows SAP customers to bring their SAP + Non-SAP and Structured + Unstructured Data together in one platform that unifies data governance, analytics, and AI. We will deep-dive on two new concepts:

  1. Data Products which enable governance and sharing organizations data assets while preserving the business context
  2. Intelligent Applications which bring together data analytics and embedded AI together.
  • When: June 12, 2025, at 11 AM EST / 5 PM CET
  • Speakers:
    • Ralph Richter (Chief Enterprise Architect, SAP)
    • Gaurish Dessai (Principal Enterprise Architect, SAP)

📅Session 4: Enterprise Architects’ Guide on SAP BW Modernization

We then Discover SAP’s BW modernization assessment, which outlines how BW can integrate into the BDC ecosystem and support your digital transformation journey. You will get a complete understanding of how you can transform your SAP Business Warehouse landscape.

  • When: June 19, 2025, at 11 AM EST / 5 PM CET
  • Speakers:
    • Jason Luo (Principal Enterprise Architect, SAP)
    • Gaurish Dessai (Principal Enterprise Architect, SAP)

📅Session 5: Planning Your Transition Paths to SAP Business Data Cloud

Transitioning to SAP Business Data Cloud is crucial for harmonizing cloud operations, enabling faster product innovation cycles, and improving resilience. By transitioning to SAP Business Data Cloud, organizations can leverage a unified data platform that supports impactful decision-making and drives innovation. This session will guide you through the strategic roadmap and practical steps to ensure a smooth and successful transition. You’ll also learn about a range of complimentary SAP assessments and services designed to support your journey and facilitate smooth collaboration as you move to the Business Data Cloud.

When: June 26th, 2025, at 11 AM EST / 5 PM CET

  • Speakers:
    • Naveen Dumpa (Enterprise Architect, SAP)
    • Jason Luo (Principal Enterprise Architect, SAP)

📅Session 6: SAP Business Data Cloud and Databricks – Stronger Together

We are then joined by our partner’s Databricks to talk about the details of how SAP and Databricks partnership is a gamechanger for organizations who are looking to transform the data. At the core of this collaboration is SAP Databricks on Business Data Cloud, a first party offering that brings Databricks’ leading Data Science, Data Warehousing, and AI capabilities into the SAP ecosystem. With SAP data natively accessible in Databricks through Unity Catalog and enriched with business-ready metadata, this solution enables customers to seamlessly harness the power of their most valuable data—SAP—on the industry's best data platform, delivering both immediate insights and long-term transformation.

  • When: July 10, 2025, at 11 AM EST / 5 PM CET
  • Speakers:
    • Qi Su (Solution Architect, Databricks)
    • Michelle Wiktorsson (Manager, Field Engineering, Databricks)
    • Habeebuddin Mohammed (Senior Director, SAP)

📅Session 7: SAP Business Data Cloud & Databricks in Action with Demos

See SAP Business Data Cloud and Databricks in action. Join us for the detailed demos of BDC Capabilities and Databricks to understand how this all comes alive together!

  • When : July 17th at 11 AM EST / 5 PM CET

Speakers:

  • Michelle Wiktorsson (Manager, Field Engineering, Databricks)
  • Gaurish Dessai (Principal Enterprise Architect, SAP)
  • Rithivika Acharya (Senior Product Specialist, SAP Business Data Cloud )
  • Peter Schmidt (Chief Solution Architect, SAP)

📅Session 8: SAP BDC in Review – Insights, Learnings, and the Road Ahead

 Join us for the final wrap-up session of the SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), where we’ll bring together key insights from across all sessions—including the architectural foundations of BDC, SAP’s strategic partnership with Databricks, BW modernization strategies, live demos, and practical transition guidance. This interactive discussion will reflect on lessons learned, highlight real-world applications of BDC, and explore how organizations can become truly data-driven by leveraging a unified, governed, and AI-ready data platform.

  • When: July 24, 2025, at 11 AM EST / 5 PM CET
  • Speakers:
    • Paul Kurchina
    • Timo Elliott
    • Ralph Richter
    • Gaurish Dessai
    • Prakash Naduri 

Check out the registration page for the whole series using this link : https://go4.events.sap.com/shift-eaa-bdc-webinar/en_us/home.html

From building a data-driven organization to unleashing AI capabilities, modernizing SAP BW, partnering with Databricks, and planning your transition to SAP BDC — each session is packed with insights, demos, and practical advice. Whether you’re just starting or looking to accelerate your data transformation, this series is your ultimate guide to unlocking new possibilities with SAP.

Register now and get ready to transform your data future!! 

If there are any questions you want answered by the experts during our live sessions, please feel free to leave them in the comments below!!

Registration link for the whole series: https://go4.events.sap.com/shift-eaa-bdc-webinar/en_us/home.html
